question_text: A solid metal cube has a mass of 2,700 g and a volume of 100 cm³. A hollow sphere made of the same metal has a volume of 200 cm³ but only contains 1,350 g of metal (the rest is air inside). Which statement best explains the difference?

options.label: A
options.text: The sphere has a different density because it contains air, which has a lower density than metal.

options.label: B
options.text: The cube and sphere have the same density because they are made of the same metal.

options.label: C
options.text: The sphere has a higher density than the cube because it has a larger volume.

options.label: D
options.text: Density cannot be compared between objects of different shapes.

explanation: The cube's density is 27 g/cm³ (pure metal), but the sphere's overall density is only 6.75 g/cm³ because the total volume includes air spaces. Density measures the mass of material in a given space; when there's air inside, the average density decreases.
